Schematic of a two-period production planning network: two gray circles labelled 1 and 2. Up-arrows show production x_1, x_2; down-arrows show demands 10 and 4; horizontal arrows show inventory s_0 in, s_1 between nodes, s_2 out. Rows labelled Production, Inventory, Demand.
figs/modeling-sums-tikz01.png: Schematic of a multi-period production network: gray circles labelled 1, 2, ..., T-1, T. Up-arrows show production x_t; down-arrows show demand d_t; horizontal arrows show inventory s_0 in, s_1, ..., s_{T-1} between nodes, s_T out. Rows labelled Production, Inventory, Demand.
figs/modeling-sums-tikz02.png: Bipartite assignment diagram: three people P1, P2, P3 drawn as stick-figure icons in blue circles on the left and three tasks T1, T2, T3 drawn as clipboard icons in red squares on the right, with lines connecting every person to every task.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz01.png: Undirected network with six numbered circular nodes: edges join 6-4, 4-5, 4-3, 5-1, 5-2, 3-2, and 2-1.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz02.png: Directed version of the same six-node network: curved arrows show arc directions, including a two-way pair of arcs between nodes 6 and 4 and a cycle through nodes 5, 1, 2, and 3.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz03.png: Transshipment network: supply nodes W1 (+30) and W2 (+20) on the left, transshipment nodes D1 and D2 in the middle, and demand nodes S1 (-20), S2 (-15), S3 (-15) on the right. Arcs are labeled with unit costs: W1 to D1 costs 4, W1 to D2 costs 6, W2 to D1 costs 5, W2 to D2 costs 4, D1 to S1 costs 2, D1 to S2 costs 3, D2 to S2 costs 1, and D2 to S3 costs 2.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz04.png: Directed network with eight nodes labeled a through h arranged left to right. Blue numbers give node supplies (+10 at a, -5 at b, +5 at d, 0 at c and e, -5 at g and h) and red numbers on the arcs give unit costs.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz05.png: Capacity network: green source s on the left, red sink t on the right, intermediate nodes a, b, c, d, e. Orange numbers give arc capacities: s-a 4, s-b 2, s-c 8, a-d 2, a-c 5, b-c 6, b-e 9, c-d 1, c-t 4, c-e 6, d-t 7, e-t 5.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz06.png: Directed max-flow network with optimum shown: green source s (left), red sink t (right), intermediate nodes a, b, c, d, e. Arcs are labelled with capacity (orange) and optimal flow (bold black); total source outflow equals 12.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz07.png: The same s-t capacity network with a wide gray diagonal band crossing four arcs; circled numbers 2, 1, 4, and 5 mark the capacities of the crossed arcs, illustrating an s-t cut of total capacity 12.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz08.png: Flow network with four blue circular nodes: arcs 3 to 1 with capacity 15, 1 to 2 with capacity 20, 2 to 4 with capacity 25, and 3 to 4 with capacity 20. Boxed labels beside each node give two numbers: node 3 (0, 0), node 1 (-10, -5), node 2 (0, 0), node 4 (10, 5).
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz09.png: The same four-node network annotated with a flow solution: arcs carry interval labels [5.0, 5.0] on 3 to 1, [10.0, 0.0] on 1 to 2, [10.0, 0.0] on 2 to 4, and [-0.0, 5.0] on 3 to 4, with boxed node labels [-10, -5], [0, 0], [0, 0], and [10, 5].
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz10.png: Disaster-relief transportation network: warehouse rectangles W1-W5 (inventories 200, 300, 150, 250, 180), distribution circles D1, D2, D3, and a central diamond Hub. Directed arcs labelled with capacities route supplies through distribution centers to the Hub.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz11.png: Directed max-flow exercise network: green source s and red sink t with intermediate nodes v1, v2, v3, v4. Arc capacities: s to v1 is 11, s to v2 is 8, between v1 and v2 are 10 and 1, v1 to v3 is 12, v3 to v2 is 4, v4 to v3 is 7, v2 to v4 is 11, v3 to t is 15, v4 to t is 4.
figs/modeling-sums-continued-tikz12.png: Six-node directed network for a min-cost flow exercise: nodes v1 through v6 with blue supply values (6 at v1, -5 at v2, -3 at v3, -6 at v4, -2 at v5, 10 at v6). Each arc shows its capacity in black and a red cost value, for example v5 to v1 has capacity 11 and cost 4.
figs/Section2-tikz01.png: Two-dimensional plot of the feasible region for max 2x_1+5x_2 with vertices (0,0), (9,0), (6,5), (0,8), bounded by x_1+2x_2<=16 and 5x_1+3x_2<=45 (red); an inset table lists the objective value at each vertex (0, 18, 37, 40).
figs/Section2-tikz02.png: Graphical method plot on x1-x2 axes running to about 90: red constraint lines bound a shaded feasible region with blue corner points labeled (0,30), (30,20), and (35,10); gray dashed level lines of the objective sweep across the region toward the optimal vertex.
figs/Section2-tikz03.png: Feasible region plot on x-y axes: blue shaded polygon bounded by red constraint lines 2x + y = 4 and x + 3y = 6 and the axes, with the optimal vertex labeled (1.2, 1.6); dashed gray objective level lines increase toward that vertex.
figs/Section2-tikz04.png: Blue shaded triangular feasible region on x1-x2 axes bounded by three lines labeled -x1 + 2x2 <= 3, x1 + 2x2 >= 3, and 2x1 + x2 <= 5.
figs/Section2-tikz05.png: Graphical method plot: shaded feasible region with blue corner points (0,30), (30,20), and (35,10); gray dashed objective level lines labeled z = 20, 40, 60, 80 sweep the region, showing the optimum at a corner.
figs/Section2-tikz06.png: Feasible region on x-y axes: blue shaded polygon with corners at (0,0), (0,2), the intersection of 2x + y = 4 and x + 3y = 6, and (2,0)?; dashed level lines labeled c = 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 increase across the region.
figs/Section2-tikz07.png: Infeasible LP illustration: constraint boundaries 2x1 + x2 <= 12 (blue) and x1 + x2 <= 8 (red) shade toward the origin while x2 >= 5 (orange) and x1 >= 7 (green) shade away from it; the note 'No feasible region' marks the empty intersection.
figs/Section2-tikz08.png: Unbounded feasible region shaded blue: bounded below by lines 5x1 + 2x2 >= 10 (green) and x1 + 3x2 >= 6 (red), above by x2 <= 4 (magenta), with labeled points (0,4), (1.38,1.54), and (6,0); the region extends to the right without bound.
figs/Section2-tikz09.png: Unbounded feasible region: blue shaded area above two red constraint boundaries meeting at labeled corner points (0,2) and (3,1); gray dashed level lines of the objective x1 + 3x2 = z increase into the unbounded region, so the LP is unbounded.
figs/Section2-tikz10.png: The same unbounded feasible region with a different objective: dashed level lines of 3x1 + x2 = z now achieve their minimum at a corner point, showing that an LP over an unbounded region can still have a finite optimum. Corner points (0,2) and (3,1) are marked.
figs/Section2-tikz11.png: Cone-shaped feasible region on x1-x2 axes: blue shading between the lines s1 = 0 (red, lower boundary) and s3 = 0 (orange, upper boundary), with the line s2 = 0 (teal) crossing above; red square markers sit at (0,0), (0,1), and (2,3).
figs/Section2-tikz12.png: The same cone-shaped region between s1 = 0 and s3 = 0 with s2 = 0 above, shaded blue and marked with red squares at (0,0), (0,1), and (2,3), used to discuss which basic solutions are feasible.
figs/Section2-tikz13.png: The same recession cone between s1 = 0 and s3 = 0 with the line x1 + x2 = 1 crossing it, used to identify the extreme rays of the cone.
figs/Section2-tikz14.png: The same recession cone between s1 = 0 and s3 = 0 with the line x1 + x2 = 1 crossing it, used to identify the extreme rays of the cone.
figs/Section2-tikz15.png: Zoomed view of the normalization segment: the line x1 + x2 = 1 crosses the shaded cone between s1 = 0 and s3 = 0, and two red square points mark where the extreme rays meet the line.
figs/formalize-LP-tikz01.png: Schematic of a convex combination on a 2D plane: two black points x and y are joined by a line segment, with a red point z placed one-third of the way from y to x to illustrate z = lambda*x + (1-lambda)*y for lambda = 2/3.
figs/formalize-LP-tikz03.png: Two side-by-side shapes contrasting a convex set (a tilted gray ellipse) with a non-convex set (a curvy gray blob with a notch); a chord between two interior dots leaves the second shape, demonstrating non-convexity.
figs/formalize-LP-tikz10.png: One-dimensional number line showing c^T z between c^T x and c^T y. Three dots mark min and max of {c^T x, c^T y} and the convex combination c^T z = lambda c^T x + (1-lambda) c^T y in between.
figs/formalize-LP-tikz11.png: Two-dimensional plot of a quadrilateral polytope with vertices (0,0), (1,2), (3,2), (4,0). A green vertex v and two interior red points x and y each show short arrows v +/- epsilon*d, distinguishing perturbations that stay inside from those that leave the polytope.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz01.png: Eighteen-vertex housing-development graph with nodes A through R laid out on a roughly grid-like map of streets; reused from earlier in the chapter to ask whether an Euler circuit exists for the lawn inspector.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz02.png: The same 18-vertex graph G (vertices A through R) redrawn with vertices scattered in a non-grid layout. The edge set is identical to the previous figure, illustrating that a graph's structure is independent of how it is drawn.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz03.png: Schematic of Konigsberg's four landmasses drawn as labelled ellipses (North Bank, Island, East Bank, South Bank) with seven short line segments crossing between them representing the seven bridges of the puzzle.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz04.png: Graph abstraction of the Konigsberg bridges: four circular vertices labelled NB, I, EB, and SB with seven edges connecting them, including two parallel arcs between NB and I and two between I and SB to represent the duplicate bridges (a multigraph).
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz05.png: Small graph illustrating a loop: three filled-dot vertices form a triangle and a red self-loop attaches to the rightmost vertex, showing an edge that connects a vertex to itself.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz06.png: Single isolated vertex with no edges, illustrating a vertex of degree 0 in the table of degree examples.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz07.png: A vertex with one edge attached, illustrating degree 1 in the table of degree examples.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz08.png: A vertex with two edges attached (forming a V), illustrating degree 2 in the table of degree examples.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz09.png: A vertex with three edges attached, illustrating degree 3 in the table of degree examples.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz10.png: A vertex with four edges attached (one to each diagonal direction), illustrating degree 4 in the table of degree examples.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz11.png: A 3-by-4 grid graph with 12 vertices labelled A-D (top), E-H (middle), J-M (bottom). All grid edges are drawn; a path from A to M is highlighted in thick red, traversing A-E-F-G-L-M.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz12.png: The same 3-by-4 grid graph (vertices A-D, E-H, J-M) with a circuit highlighted in thick red, beginning and ending at vertex A by traversing A-E-J-K-F-B-A and similar back path, illustrating a cycle.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz13.png: A 3-by-4 grid graph with vertices A-D, E-H, J-M, but with the column-A horizontal edges deleted so that vertex A and the left column are isolated from the rest, illustrating a disconnected graph.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz14.png: Weighted graph of five U.S. cities (LA, Seattle, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas) shown as filled dots arranged across the plane, with all ten edges drawn and labelled with one-way airfares such as $70 (LA-Seattle), $150 (LA-Dallas), $85 (Atlanta-Dallas), $75 (Chicago-Atlanta).
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz15.png: Initial-state diagram for Dijkstra's algorithm: seven blue circular nodes T, E, A, NB, MR, P, Y connected by weighted edges (e.g., T-A=20, A-NB=36, NB-Y=120, MR-Y=90); each node has a green-boxed tentative-distance label, all set to infinity except the destination T which is 0.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz16.png: Weighted road network for the Yakima-to-Tacoma example: seven filled-dot vertices T (Tacoma), E (Eatonville), A (Auburn), NB (North Bend), MR (Mount Rainier), P (Packwood), Y (Yakima) with edges labelled by travel times in minutes (e.g., T-A=20, A-NB=36, NB-Y=104, MR-Y=96, P-Y=76).
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz17.png: Yakima-to-Tacoma road network as before, now with the destination Y annotated [0] in red to mark it as the starting vertex of Dijkstra's algorithm with tentative distance zero.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz18.png: Yakima example after Dijkstra step 2: tentative distances [104] at NB, [96] at MR, [76] at P are recorded in red next to each neighbour of Y; Y still labelled [0]; remaining vertices T, E, A are unlabelled.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz19.png: Yakima example after the second iteration: P is the current vertex; E now has tentative distance [172] (via P), and Y is shown struck-through (visited); other labels [104] at NB, [96] at MR, [76] at P remain.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz20.png: Yakima example after the third iteration: A has tentative distance [175] (via MR); P and Y appear struck-through (visited); NB still [104], MR [96], E [172].
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz21.png: Yakima example after the fourth iteration: A's distance is updated to [140] via NB; NB, MR, P, Y all struck-through; E still [172].
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz22.png: Yakima example after the fifth iteration: T receives tentative distance [160] via A; A, NB, MR, P, Y are struck-through, leaving E [172] still active. The shortest path Y-NB-A-T of 160 minutes is now determined.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz23.png: A 4-vertex spanning tree drawn as filled dots at corners of a small region, connected by three edges forming a Y-shape (top-left to top vertex, bottom-right to middle, top to bottom-right), illustrating one possible spanning tree on four vertices.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz24.png: A 4-vertex spanning tree with three edges arranged as a path: middle vertex linked to top-right, top-right linked to bottom-right, and bottom-right linked to bottom-left, showing another spanning-tree configuration on four vertices.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz25.png: Composite figure of five small spanning trees with n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 vertices arranged left-to-right; each tree has exactly n-1 edges, demonstrating that a spanning tree on n vertices uses n-1 edges.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz26.png: n = 3 case from the spanning-tree examples: three labelled vertices A, B, C connected by two edges (A-B and B-C) forming a path, with exactly n-1 = 2 edges.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz27.png: n = 4 case from the spanning-tree examples: a star tree with center B connected to leaves A, C, and D by three edges, illustrating n-1 = 3 edges on n = 4 vertices.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz28.png: Five small spanning trees side by side on n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 vertices. Yellow circular nodes labelled A through E are connected by exactly n-1 edges in each, supporting the lemma that a tree on n vertices has n-1 edges.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz29.png: Single yellow circular node labelled A drawn in isolation, illustrating the base case of the induction proof: a tree with one vertex and zero edges satisfies |E| = n - 1 = 0.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz31.png: Pseudocode block titled 'Kruskal's Algorithm (Descriptive Version)'. Initialize T to empty; repeatedly pick the cheapest unused edge, skipping any edge that would create a cycle; stop once T contains |V|-1 edges, forming a minimum spanning tree of G.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz32.png: Five-vertex graph with cities A, B, C, D, E as black dots, fully connected with dollar-cost edge labels (AB=4, AE=5, AC=8, DC=7, plus higher cross-edges). Edges AB, AE, AC, DC are thick red, marking the MST built by Prim's algorithm starting at A.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz35.png: Undirected weighted graph with six yellow circular vertices A, B, C, E, F, G arranged hexagonally. Every pair is joined by an edge labelled with its integer weight (e.g. AB=11, BG=13, AE=14, EF=16, AC=33). Input graph for the Kruskal exercise.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz36.png: Python code listing 'Loading the graph into NetworkX' that builds the prior 6-vertex weighted graph in NetworkX: it imports nx, creates an undirected Graph, adds nodes A,B,C,E,F,G, and feeds the 15 weighted edges via G.add_weighted_edges_from(edges).
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz37.png: Five-vertex graph with cities A, B, C, D, E as black dots, fully connected with dollar-cost edge labels (AB=4, AE=5, AC=8, DC=7, plus higher cross-edges). Edges AB, AE, AC, DC are thick red, marking the MST built by Prim's algorithm starting at A.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz38.png: Same five-city phone-line graph (A, B, C, D, E) used to illustrate Prim's algorithm. Edges are labelled with dollar costs and the spanning tree edges AB, AE, AC, DC are drawn in thick red while the remaining inter-city edges are thin black.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz39.png: Boxed figure for Prim's algorithm: 7-vertex graph with vertices a, b, c, d, e, f, g as red circles. The minimum spanning tree edges d-a, d-f, a-b, b-e, e-c, e-g are drawn thick red; remaining edges are thin black with integer weights.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz40.png: Seven-vertex weighted graph for the shortest-path exercise. Vertices A, B, C, D, E, F, G in a roughly diamond layout with integer edge weights (AB=1, AC=4, BD=3, CD=2, DE=2, DF=4, EG=7, FG=6, plus chords). Goal: find the shortest path from A to G.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz41.png: Airfare table giving one-way dollar fares between Seattle, Honolulu, London, Moscow, and Cairo (e.g. Seattle-Honolulu $159, Honolulu-London $830, London-Moscow $245, Moscow-Cairo $329). Used as input for building a weighted graph.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz42.png: Small undirected graph with five black-dot vertices arranged in two rows, with four edges (square plus a diagonal extension). One of three example graphs presented side-by-side asking 'Which of these graphs are connected?'.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz43.png: Small undirected graph with five black-dot vertices; edges form one triangle on the right plus a separate vertical segment on the left, leaving the graph in two components. One of three connectivity examples.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz44.png: Small undirected graph with five black-dot vertices fully linked through a series of edges so all vertices belong to one component. The third option in the 'Which of these graphs are connected?' exercise.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz45.png: Eight-vertex graph drawn on a 3-by-3 grid layout with three short horizontal segments stacked at top, middle, and bottom; the components are not all linked, illustrating a disconnected graph for the second connectivity question.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz46.png: Eight-vertex graph on a 3-by-3 grid layout where a central vertex (1.5,1) has high degree, connecting most of the graph; one corner pair remains separated. Used for the second 'Which of these graphs are connected?' question.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz47.png: Eight-vertex graph with edges forming a connected outer cycle plus several interior diagonals; the third option in the second connectivity exercise.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz49.png: Weighted graph on five labelled vertices A, B, C, D, E in a pentagonal layout, fully connected by ten edges with dollar-cost labels (e.g. CD=$5.6, CA=$5.1, BC=$4.3, AE=$4.4, CE=$4.0); used to plan a minimum-cost sonet ring.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz51.png: Four-vertex undirected graph with B at (0,0), C at (4,0), D at (2,2), A at (2,4) and five edges BC, BD, BA, CD, CA forming a kite; left panel of an example illustrating an Euler path CABDCB.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz52.png: Same four-vertex kite graph (B, C, D, A) as the left panel, but with directed arrows along the Euler path CABDCB; edges are numbered 1 through 5 in the order they are traversed.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz53.png: Six-vertex graph with vertices A, B, C, D, E, F in a roughly diamond layout and nine undirected edges; the example asks the student to find an Euler circuit such as ADEACEFCBA starting and ending at A.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz54.png: Same six-vertex graph as the previous panel, redrawn with directed arrows tracing the Euler circuit AECABCFEDA and edges numbered 1 through 9 in traversal order.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz55.png: Five-vertex graph with B(0,0), C(4,0), D(2,2), A(2,4), E(6,2) and edges CE, BD, BA, CD, CA, plus a bent edge from A to C; vertices D and E have odd degree, A and C have degree 4, B has degree 2, illustrating Euler's path-but-not-circuit theorem.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz56.png: Eighteen-vertex housing-development graph with nodes A through R laid out on a roughly grid-like map of streets; reused from earlier in the chapter to ask whether an Euler circuit exists for the lawn inspector.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz57.png: Same eighteen-vertex housing-development graph with the eight vertices of odd degree (D, B, G, N, O, H, J, P) highlighted as filled yellow circles, showing why no Euler circuit exists.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz58.png: The eighteen-vertex housing-development graph redrawn with every street represented by two parallel edges (one straight, one bent) so that every vertex now has even degree, illustrating that the snowplow's two-sides-of-the-street graph does have an Euler circuit.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz59.png: Five-vertex graph with B(0,0), A(1,2), C(1,-2), E(3,-1), D(3,1) and six edges; the original input graph for the worked Fleury's algorithm example that finds Euler circuit ADEBDCA starting at A.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz60.png: Undirected graph on five vertices A, B, C, D, E (second panel of Fleury's algorithm trace). Edge AD has been removed; remaining edges are B-D, B-E, A-C, C-D, D-E. Current vertex is D; circuit so far is AD.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz61.png: Same five-vertex graph (third panel of Fleury's algorithm trace). Edges AD and DE have now been removed, leaving B-D, B-E, A-C, C-D. From E only one option remains, so the rest of the Euler circuit ADEBDCA is forced.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz62.png: Undirected graph on seven vertices A, B, C, D, E, F, G drawn left-to-right with B/E above and C/F below. Eleven edges connect the vertices; the reader is asked to decide whether an Euler circuit exists and, if so, to find one.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz63.png: Rectangular 4-by-3 grid graph (twelve vertices, seventeen grid edges, no duplicated edges shown). This is the original graph used to illustrate three different eulerizations in Example 11.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz64.png: First eulerization of the 4-by-3 grid: five red bend-left arcs duplicate edges in the middle row and adjacent rows, giving every vertex even degree using five extra edges so an Euler circuit becomes possible.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz65.png: Second eulerization of the 4-by-3 grid using a different choice of five duplicated edges (red bend-left arcs spread across the top, middle, and bottom rows). It also uses five extra edges and yields an Euler circuit.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz66.png: Third eulerization of the 4-by-3 grid using seven duplicated edges (red bend-left arcs concentrated around the middle column). It is a valid eulerization but uses two more duplicated edges than the previous two examples.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz67.png: Four-vertex undirected graph with B at (0,0), C at (4,0), D at (2,2), A at (2,4) and five edges BC, BD, BA, CD, CA forming a kite; left panel of an example illustrating an Euler path CABDCB.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz68.png: 4-by-3 grid graph with twelve vertices labelled A-D (top row), E-H (middle), J-M (bottom). Red edges trace the Hamiltonian circuit ABFGCDHMLKJEA, showing a circuit that visits every vertex once but does not use every edge.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz69.png: Five-vertex graph with B(0,0), C(4,0), D(2,2), A(2,4), E(6,2) and edges CE, BD, BA, CD, CA, plus a bent edge from A to C; vertices D and E have odd degree, A and C have degree 4, B has degree 2, illustrating Euler's path-but-not-circuit theorem.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz70.png: Weighted complete graph on five cities (Home/Seattle, LA, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas) with all ten edges labelled by airfare in dollars (e.g., LA-Seattle $70, Chicago-Atlanta $75, Atlanta-Dallas $85). Used to motivate the Traveling Salesman Problem.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz71.png: Same four-vertex weighted graph (A, B, C, D with weights 1, 2, 4, 8, 9, 13) used again to illustrate the Repeated Nearest Neighbor Algorithm running NNA from each starting vertex.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz73.png: Same four-vertex weighted graph (A, B, C, D with weights 1, 2, 4, 8, 9, 13) used again to illustrate the Repeated Nearest Neighbor Algorithm running NNA from each starting vertex.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz74.png: Five-city air travel graph (Seattle, LA, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas) with the NNA tour Seattle-LA-Chicago-Atlanta-Dallas-Seattle highlighted in red. The highlighted total cost is $450, which happens to be optimal here.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz75.png: Same four-vertex weighted graph (A, B, C, D with weights 1, 2, 4, 8, 9, 13) used again to illustrate the Repeated Nearest Neighbor Algorithm running NNA from each starting vertex.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz76.png: Sorted Edges algorithm step 1 on the four-vertex graph: only the cheapest edge AD (weight 1) is highlighted in red.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz77.png: Sorted Edges step 2 on the same four-vertex graph: edges AD (weight 1) and AC (weight 2) are now highlighted in red.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz78.png: Sorted Edges rejected case (BAD) on the four-vertex graph: adding edge AB to the previously selected AD and AC would give vertex A degree 3, so this third edge is rejected.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz79.png: Sorted Edges second rejected case (BAD) on the four-vertex graph: adding edge CD to AD and AC would close the partial circuit ACDA without including vertex B, so CD is rejected.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz80.png: Sorted Edges accepted case (GOOD) on the four-vertex graph: edge BD (weight 9) is added to AD and AC because it neither creates a degree-3 vertex nor closes a sub-circuit.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz81.png: Final Sorted Edges circuit on the four-vertex graph: edges AD, AC, BD, and BC are highlighted, forming the Hamiltonian circuit ACBDA with total weight 25 (not optimal; the optimum ACDBA has weight 23).
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz84.png: Oregon-cities graph after seven Sorted-Edges selections: the previous four edges plus Portland-Seaside (78), Newport-Eugene (91), and Ashland-Crater Lake (108). Salem and Corvallis already have degree 2.
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz85.png: Oregon-cities graph showing the completed Sorted-Edges Hamiltonian circuit: ten edges connect the cities in the tour Portland, Salem, Corvallis, Eugene, Newport, Bend, Ashland, Crater Lake, Astoria, Seaside, Portland (1241 miles).
figs/graphtheory-dor1-tikz86.png: Weighted graph on six vertices A, B, C, E, F, G arranged left to right with fifteen edges (weights from 11 to 45). Reader is asked to apply the Sorted Edges algorithm to find a Hamiltonian circuit.
figs/equations-and-lines-new-tikz01.png: Two-dimensional plot of the line y = 3x + 2 on x-y axes with a gridded background. Three labelled points (-1,-1), (0,2), and (1,5) lie on a blue line of slope 3 with y-intercept 2.
figs/equations-and-lines-new-tikz02.png: Two-dimensional plot of the line 2x + y = 4 on gridded x-y axes. A blue line passes through three labelled points (-1,6), (0,4), and (1,2), illustrating slope -2 and y-intercept 4.
figs/equations-and-lines-new-tikz05.png: Two-dimensional plot of the horizontal line y = 3 on gridded x-y axes. The blue line is drawn parallel to the x-axis through the labelled y-intercept (0,3).
figs/equations-and-lines-new-tikz06.png: Two-dimensional plot of the vertical line x = -2 on gridded x-y axes. The blue line is drawn parallel to the y-axis through the labelled x-intercept (-2,0).
figs/equations-and-lines-new-tikz07.png: Two-dimensional plot illustrating the slope of the line through (-2,3) and (4,-1) on x-y axes. A blue line connects the two labelled points; green arrows mark a vertical drop of -4 and a horizontal run of 6, giving slope -2/3.
figs/equations-and-lines-new-tikz08.png: Two-dimensional plot of the line through (1,2) with slope -3/4 on gridded x-y axes. A blue line passes through the labelled points (1,2) and (5,-1); green arrows indicate a vertical change of -3 and horizontal change of 4.
figs/equations-and-lines-new-tikz09.png: Two-dimensional plot of an equilibrium point. The blue upward supply line y = 3.5x - 14 and the red downward demand line y = -2.5x + 34 intersect at the labelled green point (8,14), with dashed lines dropping to Quantity = 8 and Price = 14.
figs/equations-and-lines-new-tikz10.png: Two-dimensional plot of a break-even point. The blue revenue line R = 5x and the red cost line C = 3x + 12 intersect at the labelled green point (6,30), with dashed lines marking Quantity = 6 and Cost/Revenue = 30.
figs/systemsofequationsGeometry-tikz01.p: Three side-by-side 2D plots showing how two lines can intersect: 'One Solution' (crossing at a single point), 'No Solutions' (parallel lines), and 'Infinitely Many Solutions' (a single line representing two coincident equations).
figs/systemsofequationsGeometry-tikz02.p: Two-dimensional plot of the system x+y=3 and y-x=5: a green line and a blue line crossing at the labelled point (x,y) = (-1,4), with helper lines dropped to the x- and y-axes to show the unique solution.
figs/systemsofequationsGeometry-tikz03.p: Two side-by-side two-dimensional plots showing three lines in the plane: on the left, three lines that pairwise intersect but share no common point ('No Solution'); on the right, three lines all passing through a single marked point ('One Solution').
figs/RnVectorsRn-tikz01.png: Two-dimensional plot of the xy-plane showing two points P = (2,1) and Q = (-3,4) marked as red dots, with light helper lines dropped to the axes to indicate each point's coordinates.
figs/RnVectorsRn-tikz02.png: Three-dimensional diagram of the position vector 0P: a thick blue arrow from the origin along the x-, y-, z-axes to a red point P=(p_1,p_2,p_3), labelled with the column-vector form [p_1,p_2,p_3]^T.
figs/RnVectorsRn-tikz03.png: Three-dimensional diagram with two parallel blue arrows of equal length and direction: the position vector 0P from the origin and a translated copy AB from point A to point B, illustrating that translating a vector preserves its identity.
figs/RnVectorsAdditionMeaning-tikz01.png: Two-dimensional vector diagram illustrating the tip-to-tail rule for vector addition: arrow u along the x-axis, arrow v starting at the tip of u and going to the upper right, and a third arrow from the tail of u to the tip of v labelled u+v.
figs/RnVectorsAdditionMeaning-tikz02.png: Two-dimensional sketch of vectors u (blue, up and right) and v (red, down and right) drawn as separate arrows, presented as the setup for graphing the linear combinations u+2v and u-1/2 v.
figs/RnVectorsAdditionMeaning-tikz03.png: Two-dimensional vector diagram of u+v drawn tip-to-tail: blue u from the origin to the upper right, red v continuing from the tip of u, and the purple resultant u+v drawn from the tail of u to the tip of v.
figs/RnVectorsAdditionMeaning-tikz04.png: Two-dimensional vector diagram of u-v: blue u from the origin, red -v starting at the tip of u and pointing in the reverse direction of v, and a purple arrow from the origin to the tip of -v representing u-v=u+(-v).
figs/RnVectorsScalarMultMeaning-tikz01.p: Two-dimensional sketch of vectors u (blue, up and right) and v (red, down and right) drawn as separate arrows, presented as the setup for graphing the linear combinations u+2v and u-1/2 v.
figs/RnVectorsScalarMultMeaning-tikz02.p: Two-dimensional diagram showing scalar multiples of two vectors: blue vector u and its negative -u (same length, opposite direction), plus red vector v together with 2v (twice as long, same direction) and -1/2 v (half-length, reversed).
figs/RnVectorsScalarMultMeaning-tikz03.p: Two-dimensional sketch of vectors u (blue, up and right) and v (red, down and right) drawn as separate arrows, presented as the setup for graphing the linear combinations u+2v and u-1/2 v.
figs/RnVectorsScalarMultMeaning-tikz04.p: Two-dimensional vector diagram of the linear combination u + 2v: blue vector u from the origin, red vector 2v drawn tip-to-tail starting at u's head, and the purple resultant u+2v from the origin to the final tip.
figs/RnVectorsScalarMultMeaning-tikz05.p: Two-dimensional vector diagram of the linear combination u - 1/2 v: blue u from the origin, the short red vector -1/2 v placed tip-to-tail at u's head, and the purple resultant u - 1/2 v drawn from the origin to the final tip.
figs/RnVectorsDotProductSignificance-tik: Two-dimensional sketch of two vectors emanating from a common origin: a blue vector v pointing up and to the right and a red vector u pointing down and to the right, with the included angle theta marked between them.
figs-conv/dijkstra4.png: A later step of Dijkstra's algorithm showing progress through the graph with more nodes visited and updated distance labels from the source.
figs-conv/LP-figure.png: A linear programming visualization showing a light blue hexagonal feasible region with labeled vertices, an objective direction arrow, and a red objective function line.
figs-conv/excel-solver-answer-report.jpg: Microsoft Excel Solver Answer Report showing optimization results with objective cell value, decision variable values, and constraint status information.
figs-conv/dijkstra5.png: Dijkstra's algorithm in progress with all nodes reached. The shortest path tree is shown in red, with final distance labels indicating the algorithm has found a path to all nodes.
figs-conv/excel-solver-method.jpg: Excel Solver dropdown menu showing three solving method options: GRG Nonlinear, Simplex LP (selected), and Evolutionary.
figs-conv/risk-plot.png: Graph showing an inventory risk function where risk cost increases rapidly and asymptotically approaches a maximum as inventory level increases.
figs-conv/dijkstra7.png: Dijkstra's algorithm final iteration showing the completed shortest path tree with all nodes labeled with their final distances from the source.
figs-conv/wiki-File-knapsack.png: Illustration of the knapsack problem: a backpack with 15 kg capacity and a collection of boxes with different weights and dollar values, asking which boxes to select to maximize value.
figs-conv/dijkstra6.png: Dijkstra's algorithm nearing completion with the path to the final node confirmed. The red highlighted edges show the shortest path tree from the source.
figs-conv/dijkstra-soln.png: A graph after running Dijkstra's algorithm, with the shortest path tree highlighted showing minimum distance paths from the source to all other nodes.
figs-conv/GraphPictureDot.png: A side-by-side comparison showing an aerial view of a residential neighborhood with red dots marking intersections, and the corresponding abstract graph representation with labeled nodes.
figs-conv/dijkstra2.png: A weighted graph showing a subsequent step of Dijkstra's algorithm where nodes a and b are highlighted, and node d has updated tentative distance 7.
figs-conv/new-york-tolls.png: Map of New York City's five boroughs showing a transportation network with red dashed toll roads and a green no-toll route connecting Staten Island, Brooklyn, and Queens.
figs-conv/dijkstra3.png: An intermediate step of Dijkstra's algorithm showing a weighted graph with visited nodes and edges highlighted in red, with distance labels showing current shortest distances from the source.
figs-conv/dijkstra1.png: A weighted graph showing the initial step of Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm with node a as source with distance 0, node b with tentative distance 2, and other nodes showing infinity.
figs-conv/MIP-feasible-region.png: A mixed-integer programming feasible region with blue shading showing the LP relaxation, vertical dark green bars indicating where integer restrictions apply.
figs-conv/dijkstra0.png: A weighted graph with 7 nodes used to demonstrate Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm, with blue edge weights shown.
figs-conv/desmos-graph-slacks.png: An LP feasible region with vertices labeled and slack variable constraints labeled in different colors, showing how each constraint defines a boundary.
figs-conv/StripPacking2.png: A feasible strip packing solution showing rectangles packed into the strip with coordinate positions listed for each rectangle.
figs-conv/excel-sensitivity-report.jpg: A Microsoft Excel Solver sensitivity analysis report showing Variable Cells and Constraints sections with columns for Final Value, Reduced Cost, and Shadow Price.
figs-conv/excel-solver-solve.jpg: Excel Solver Parameters dialog box configured with objective cell, variable cells, constraints, non-negativity option, and Simplex LP method selected.
figs-conv/jssp.png: Gantt chart showing a job shop scheduling solution with three machines processing five jobs over time. Colored horizontal bars represent job operations on each machine, demonstrating how jobs are sequenced to minimize makespan.
figs-conv/tikz-Illustration1.png: Layout of districts showing numbered regions and possible locations for fire stations.
figs-conv/tikz-Illustration3.png: Graph representation of fire station problem showing nodes connected to chosen fire station nodes by edges.
figs-conv/StripPacking1.png: A strip packing diagram showing several gray rectangles of varying sizes packed within a vertical strip of width W, with a dotted horizontal line at height H indicating the current maximum height.
figs-conv/excel-sensitivity.jpg: A Microsoft Excel Solver Results dialog box showing a successful optimization, with options to keep the solution or restore original values, and report type selections.
figs-conv/pareto-curve.png: A Pareto front curve showing the trade-off between Total Cost and Maximum Risk Cost, demonstrating diminishing returns as one objective improves at the expense of the other.
figs-conv/excel-setup.jpg: An Excel spreadsheet setup for a linear programming problem with decision variables in yellow cells. Constraints are defined with coefficients, totals, and right-hand side values.
figs-conv/StripPacking0.png: Strip packing problem input showing four rectangles with their dimensions labeled, alongside an empty strip where they need to be packed.
figs-conv/tikz-Illustration2.png: Set cover representation of fire station problem showing which districts are covered by each potential fire station location.
figs-conv/sensitivity-objective.jpg: Sensitivity analysis visualization showing how the optimal solution changes as objective function coefficients vary.
figs-conv/facility-location.png: A network diagram for facility location showing three potential distribution centers with fixed costs and capacities, connected to four customer locations with demand values and shipping costs.
figs-conv/jssp-duplo.png: Gantt chart showing a Job Shop Scheduling Problem solution with three machines and four jobs represented by colored bars over a time horizon of 0 to 12 units.
figs-conv/excel-solver-button.jpg: Excel ribbon Data tab with the Solver button highlighted in the Analysis Tools section on the far right.
figs-conv/excel-solver-sensitivity.jpg: Microsoft Excel Solver Sensitivity Report displaying variable cells with final values, reduced costs, objective coefficients, and allowable increase/decrease ranges.
figs-conv/excel-solver-select.jpg: Excel Solver Results dialog box indicating a solution was found with all constraints satisfied, showing options for reports including Answer, Sensitivity, and Limits.
figs-conv/LP-feasible-region.png: A two-dimensional plot showing a blue shaded polygonal feasible region bounded by green dashed constraint lines, illustrating the feasible set of a linear programming problem.
figs-conv/IP-feasible-region.png: An integer programming feasible region showing a blue shaded LP relaxation polytope with green dots highlighting the integer feasible points within the region.
figs-conv/GraphPicture.png: An aerial photograph of a residential neighborhood showing houses arranged in subdivisions with streets forming a network pattern, used to illustrate real-world applications of graph theory.
figs-conv/unbounded-desmos-simplex.png: A visualization of an unbounded LP showing a feasible region extending infinitely upward, demonstrating an unbounded optimal objective value.
figs-conv/pwl-plot.png: Plot of a piecewise linear function composed of several line segments with breakpoints marked on the horizontal axis.
figs-conv/GraphExercise2.png: A stylized map showing islands or landmasses in cyan connected by bridges shown as black rectangular segments, illustrating a Konigsberg-type bridge crossing problem.
figs-conv/jssp-duplo-actual.png: Photograph of colorful Duplo building blocks arranged on a table, representing a physical demonstration of a job shop scheduling problem with blocks of different colors and sizes.
figs-conv/Konigsberg_bridges.png: A historical map of Konigsberg showing the seven bridges problem, with bridges highlighted connecting different land masses separated by the river.
figs-conv/GraphExercise1.png: Six small square grids arranged in two rows of three, each containing different patterns of black dots at various positions, representing a graph theory or combinatorics exercise.
figs-conv/excel-solver-solution.jpg: Excel spreadsheet showing the solved linear programming problem with optimal objective value, decision variable values, and constraint resources used.
figs-conv/wiki-File-Petersen-graph-3-col: Petersen graph with vertices colored using three colors (red, green, blue) demonstrating a valid 3-coloring where no adjacent vertices share the same color.
figs-conv/tree-diagram-types.png: A hierarchical classification of optimization models showing Convex models and Non-convex models as subcategories.
figs-conv/dijkstra8.png: The completed Dijkstra's algorithm result showing the final shortest path tree from the source node with all minimum distances displayed and shortest path tree edges highlighted.
figs-conv/excel-solver-layout.jpg: Excel spreadsheet layout for a linear programming problem showing the objective function using SUMPRODUCT, decision variables, objective coefficients, and constraints.
